evals.name: messy-working-tree-multi-area
evals.prompt: I've been working on a bunch of stuff today. Can you look at my changes and help me commit them? I've got changes in the auth module (new token refresh logic + tests), some test fixes for the payment module, and I updated the README with new setup instructions. Everything is unstaged.
evals.expected_output: Agent should: (1) run git status and git diff to inspect changes, (2) group changes into logical commits -- auth feature with its tests as one feat: commit, payment test fixes as a test: commit (not fix:), README as a docs: commit, (3) present the grouping plan to the user for approval before committing, (4) use conventional commit format with appropriate types and scopes

assertions.text: Agent runs git status and git diff before proposing any commits
Agent groups auth feature code and its tests together in one feat: commit (not splitting tests into a separate test: commit)
Agent separates the README change into a docs: commit
Payment module test fixes get a test: type commit (not fix: or feat:)
Agent presents the grouping plan and asks for user confirmation before executing any git commands
All commit messages follow conventional commit format: type(scope): description

evals.name: staged-changes-mixed-intent
evals.prompt: I staged 4 files before realizing they cover two different things. Can you write me a commit message? The staged files are: src/auth/session.py (new session timeout logic), tests/test_session.py (tests for the session timeout), docs/security.md (new security guide), and src/utils/logger.py (fixed a bug where log level wasn't being respected). I want to commit what's staged as-is.
evals.expected_output: Agent should: (1) run git diff --cached to inspect staged changes, (2) recognize that all 4 files are staged and the user explicitly wants to commit them as-is, (3) write a single commit message that covers the staged set -- since the user has decided to commit them together, the agent should not try to split them. For a mixed-intent staged commit, the best approach is to use the dominant change in the subject and optionally add a brief body listing the other included changes. The agent should NOT propose to unstage files.

assertions.text: Agent uses git diff --cached (not git diff) to inspect staged changes
Agent produces exactly one commit message (respects the user's decision to commit staged files as-is)
Agent does not propose to unstage or restage files
Commit message subject line is under 72 characters
Commit message uses imperative mood (e.g., 'add' not 'added' or 'adds')

evals.name: large-changeset-organization
evals.prompt: I have a messy working tree with 15 changed files across 6 directories. Help me organize these into logical commits. The changes include: new user profile page (frontend + backend + tests), a database migration for the profile table, a bug fix in the email service, updated CI config to add Python 3.12 to the test matrix, and some refactoring of the auth module to extract a helper function.
evals.expected_output: Agent should: (1) inspect the working tree, (2) identify 4-5 distinct logical groups: profile feature (frontend+backend+tests+migration), email bug fix, CI config change, auth refactor, (3) present a clear grouping plan with conventional commit messages, (4) order commits sensibly per the skill's Step 5 guidance (CI/infra first, then refactors, then features and fixes), (5) ask for confirmation. The database migration should be grouped WITH the profile feature (not as a standalone chore:). The auth refactor tests should go WITH the refactor commit.

assertions.text: Agent identifies at least 4 distinct commit groups from the described changes
CI config change gets a ci: type commit
Auth refactor gets a refactor: type commit (not feat: or fix:)
Email bug fix gets a fix: type commit
Database migration is grouped with the profile feature commit (not as a standalone chore: or separate commit)
Agent presents the plan and asks for confirmation before committing
